content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence of positive integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. You should answer QQQ queries. In each query:


You are given a positive integer MMM.
Consider all non-empty subsequences of AAA with length ≤M\le M≤M. Recall that a subsequence is any sequence that can be created by deleting zero or more elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.
For each of these subsequences, compute the bitwise XOR of its elements. Your task is to determine the sum of these values. Since this sum can be very large, compute it modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N – the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1 … AN. The third line contains an integer Q – the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains a single integer M (1 ≤ M ≤ N).
content.outputFormat: For each query output one line – the sum of XOR values of all non‑empty subsequences whose length does not exceed M, taken modulo 998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the first query, the answer is just the sum of elements of AAA (modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353), which is 1+3+5+2=111+3+5+2 = 111+3+5+2=11.
In the second query, the answer is the sum of bitwise XORs for all subsequences with length 111 or 222, which is 1+3+5+2+(1⊕3)+(1⊕5)+(1⊕2)+(3⊕5)+(3⊕2)+(5⊕2)=341+3+5+2 + (1 \oplus 3) + (1 \oplus 5) + (1 \oplus 2) + (3 \oplus 5) + (3 \oplus 2) + (5 \oplus 2) = 341+3+5+2+(1⊕3)+(1⊕5)+(1⊕2)+(3⊕5)+(3⊕2)+(5⊕2)=34.

solution.approach:
For each bit b (0 … 29) let cnt1 be the number of array elements whose b‑th bit equals 1 and cnt0 = N - cnt1. For a fixed subset size k the number of subsets of size k whose XOR has bit b equal to 1 is the number of ways to choose an odd amount of the cnt1 elements and any amount of the cnt0 elements:
    odd_b(k) = Σ_{i odd} C(cnt1, i)·C(cnt0, k-i).
Using the identity Σ_{i} (-1)^i C(cnt1,i)C(cnt0,k-i) = coeff_b[k] (the coefficient of x^k in (1‑x)^{cnt1}(1+x)^{cnt0}) we obtain
    odd_b(k) = (C(N,k) - coeff_b[k]) / 2.
All calculations are performed modulo MOD = 998244353, and division by 2 is multiplication by inv2 = (MOD+1)/2.
The polynomial coeff_b is the convolution of two sequences:
    A[i] = (-1)^i·C(cnt1,i)   (0 ≤ i ≤ cnt1)
    B[j] = C(cnt0,j)          (0 ≤ j ≤ cnt0)
Thus coeff_b = A * B, which can be computed in O(N log N) with a Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) because MOD is a suitable prime. We repeat the NTT for each of the 30 bits (30·N·log N ≈ 1.1·10^8 operations, fast enough).
After obtaining coeff_b for all k (0 … N) we compute odd_b(k) for every k, then the contribution of bit b to the answer for size k is (1<<b)·odd_b(k). Summing over all bits gives X[k] – the total XOR sum of all subsets of exact size k. A prefix array pref[M] = Σ_{k=1..M} X[k] answers each query in O(1).
Pre‑computations:
  • factorials and inverse factorials up to N for fast binomial coefficients.
  • inv2 = (MOD+1)/2.
Overall complexity: O(30·N·log N + Q) time, O(N) additional memory per bit (reused), total O(N) memory besides input.
The same algorithm is implemented in all five languages; the C++ version uses the atcoder::convolution helper, the other languages contain a hand‑written iterative NTT.
solution.timeComplexity: O(30·N·log N + Q) ≈ 1.1·10^8 operations for N = 2·10^5, plus O(Q) for answering queries.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for factorials, inverse factorials and temporary NTT buffers (reused for each bit).
